variables.sky(i): function determining whether this pixel is the sky

svc_matrix_expansion.feature_aggregation_rule: Compute openness or occlusion as the residual sky/ceiling exposure generated by vegetation and built structures across depth tiers; size and position features jointly govern the indicator.
svc_matrix_expansion.feature_matrix_note: This field explicitly encodes how the compressed SVC indicator expands into the N × 3 × K feature matrix.

operational_alignment.note: Observed IOM signatures extend beyond the canonical representational scope; this indicator requires explicit operational-lever annotation.

IND_TVF.name: Tree View Factor
IND_TVF.definition: IND_TVF (Tree View Factor) measures the proportion of tree canopy visible from a viewing point. Two operationalizations exist: (1) Simple pixel ratio: Pixels(Tree)/Total_Pixels (commonly used); (2) Cosine-altitude-weighted hemispherical view factor (Cai et al. 2023): TVF = Σ cos(Alti)·Ai·δi,green / Adome,cos. The hemispherical version is more physically meaningful for thermal/visual exposure analyses.
IND_TVF.formula: (Simple) Pixels(Tree) / Total_Pixels ; (Hemispherical, Cai et al. 2023) Σ cos(Alti) · Ai · δi,green / Adome,cos

variables.Pixels(Tree): Number of pixels classified as tree canopy in image
variables.Total_Pixels: Total pixel count of the image
variables.cos(Alti): (hemispherical) Cosine of altitude angle of direction i
variables.Ai: (hemispherical) Area of element i on the projected hemisphere
variables.δi,green: (hemispherical) Boolean indicator: 1 if direction i is occupied by green vegetation, 0 otherwise

IND_FNC.name: Fence Ratio
IND_FNC.definition: IND_FNC (Fence Ratio) belongs to the view-index (VI) system of physical-perception indicators proposed by Zhang et al. (2021). It is defined as the proportion of pixels classified as Fence (railings/barriers) within the visible area of a street view image. The paper uses a Panoptic-FPN model trained on COCO Panoptic Segmentation, which parses each street view image into 17 major physical-element categories; Fence is one of them.

IND_ASV.definition: Artificial Structure Visibility Index (ASVI): proportion of panoramic / street-view image pixels classified as artificial structures (wall, building, fence, skyscraper, bench, streetlight) by semantic segmentation. Roads are handled by a separate Road Visibility Index (RVI) and are NOT counted in ASVI.

IND_NAT.name: Naturalness Index
IND_NAT.definition: Naturalness = arctan( sum of pixel-area proportions of natural semantic classes (e.g., tree, grass, plant/flora, palm, water, rock, mountain, animal) divided by sum of pixel-area proportions of grey/artificial-infrastructure classes (e.g., building, pavement, road) ); the inverse tangent bounds the ratio to [0, pi/2). (Source: Zhang et al. 2023, 'Assessment of visual landscape quality of urban green spaces', Table 2.)
IND_NAT.formula: Naturalness = arctan( Sum_{c in NaturalSet} A_c / Sum_{c in GreySet} A_c )

IND_FRD.name: Fractal Dimension
IND_FRD.definition: IND_FRD is a quantitative measure of morphological complexity and roughness, derived from fractal geometry. In visual landscape analysis, it assesses the texture and edge complexity of skylines or vegetation patterns, typically using the box-counting method. A higher fractal dimension suggests a more complex, information-rich visual environment, often resembling natural forms. This indicator is widely used in environmental psychology to predict preference, as humans tend to prefer mid-to-high fractal complexity which mimics the structural order found in nature.

variables.N(r): Number of boxes covering the pattern

IND_SHA.name: Shade Coverage
IND_SHA.definition: IND_SHA quantifies shade exposure along a street as a sun-path-derived binary status (sunlit=1 / shaded=0) at each sampling point, obtained by overlaying the sun's azimuth/elevation track on a segmented fisheye street-view image; aggregated metrics include the sunlit ratio (mean binary status within a circular buffer, range 0-1) and cumulative sunlit hours per location.

supporting_papers.citation: Zeng, L., Lindberg, F., Zhang, X., Pan, H., & Lu, J. (2023). Road surface temperature evaluated with streetview-derived parameters in a hot and humid megacity. Urban Climate, 51, 101585.
Yang S, Chong A, Liu P, Biljecki F (2025): Thermal comfort in sight: Thermal affordance and its visual assessment for sustainable streetscape design. Building and Environment, 271: 112569.

IND_SHA.first_confirmed_by: Yang S, Chong A, Liu P, Biljecki F (2025): Thermal comfort in sight: Thermal affordance and its visual assessment for sustainable streetscape design. Building and Environment, 271: 112569.
IND_SHA.formula: Per-point: shaded = 1 if sun position overlaps non-sky elements in the segmented fisheye skydome, else 0 (sunlit). Aggregated sunlit ratio: SunlitRatio = (1/N) * SUM_{k=1..N} status_k over points within buffer; cumulative sunlit hours: SunlitHours = sum over 5-min intervals from 05:00 BJT where status=sunlit.

variables.status_k: Binary sunlit (1) / shaded (0) status at sample point k, determined by sun-position overlap with non-sky elements in the segmented fisheye image
variables.N: Number of sample points within the aggregation buffer
variables.buffer_radius: Radius of the circular aggregation buffer (e.g., 50 m)
variables.SunlitRatio: Mean of binary sunlit status within the buffer, in [0,1]
variables.SunlitHours: Cumulative sunlit hours per location, summed in 5-min increments from 05:00 BJT

IND_GIN.name: Gini Index

supporting_papers.citation: Amiruzzaman, M., Zhao, Y., Amiruzzaman, S., Karpinski, A. C., & Wu, T. H. (2023). An AI-based framework for studying visual diversity of urban neighborhoods and its relationship with socio-demographic variables. Journal of Computational Social Science, 6(1), 315–337.

Guo, X., Liu, C., Bi, S., & Tang, Y. (2025). Identification of Inequities in Green Visibility and Ways to Increase Greenery in Neighborhoods: A Case Study of Wuhan, China. Applied Sciences, 15(2), 742.

Xiao, L., Wang, W., Ren, Z., Wei, C., & He, X. (2024). Landscape Patterns of Green Spaces Drive the Availability and Spatial Fairness of Street Greenery in Changchun City, Northeastern China. Forests, 15(7), 1074.

IND_GIN.definition: Gini Index (per landscape category): a 4-step diversity statistic on the across-image distribution of a single semantic-segmentation category's pixel-proportion values within an urban neighborhood. Larger value indicates greater within-neighborhood heterogeneity of that category.
IND_GIN.formula: Step1: S_c = sum_k p_{c,k} over all images k for category c; Step2: sort p_{c,k} ascending; Step3: q_{c,k} = p_{c,k} / S_c; Step4: G_c = cumulative sum of q_{c,k}. (Amiruzzaman et al. 2023, 4-step procedure - not the canonical Lorenz-curve Gini.)

variables.p_{c,k}: Pixel proportion of category c in image k (e.g., Road, Building, Green, Terrain, Sky)
variables.S_c: Sum of proportions of category c across all images of the neighborhood
variables.q_{c,k}: Normalized probability of each sorted proportion value
variables.G_c: Per-category Gini index value (cumulative sum of sorted normalized probabilities)

IND_GIN.first_confirmed_by: Amiruzzaman, M., Zhao, Y., Amiruzzaman, S., Karpinski, A. C., & Wu, T. H. (2023). An AI-based framework for studying visual diversity of urban neighborhoods and its relationship with socio-demographic variables. Journal of Computational Social Science, 6, 315–337.

svc_matrix_expansion.feature_aggregation_rule: Aggregate the visible proportion or count of the targeted semantic class(es) across the relevant depth tiers using size-dominant features.
svc_matrix_expansion.feature_matrix_note: This field explicitly encodes how the compressed SVC indicator expands into the N × 3 × K feature matrix.

operational_alignment.note: Observed IOM signatures extend beyond the canonical representational scope; this indicator requires explicit operational-lever annotation.

IND_PCI.definition: Public-Facility Convenience Index (PCI): the ratio of the sum of pixels from 10 categories of public-service facilities to the total image pixel count in a street view image. Reflects the level of public-amenity infrastructure within a neighborhood.
IND_PCI.formula: PCI = (P_chair + P_toilet + P_bench + P_stool + P_trashcan + P_table + P_armchair + P_light + P_streetlight + P_bulletinboard) / P_all

variables.P_all: Total number of pixels in the image

IND_PCI.first_confirmed_by: Gao, L., Xiang, X., Chen, W., Nong, R., Zhang, Q., Chen, X., & Chen, Y. (2024). Research on Urban Street Spatial Quality Based on Street View Image Segmentation. Sustainability, 16(16), 7184.

IND_VAC.formula: VAC = Sum(Pixels_Sidewalk + Pixels_Streetlight + Pixels_Bench + Pixels_Path + Pixels_Stairs) / Total_Pixels

variables.x_perc: Percent of pixels in image classified as object x by PSPNet (122-class)

IND_VGD.definition: A measure of the spatial dispersion/clustering of vegetation pixels within a street-view image, derived as the ratio of joint count statistics over the percent pixel coverage, following Larkin et al. (2021) Eq. (1).

IND_IMG_CON.name: Image Contrast

supporting_papers.citation: Li, X., Zhang, C., & Li, W. (2015). Does the Visibility of Greenery Increase Perceived Safety in Urban Areas? Evidence from the Place Pulse 1.0 Dataset. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 4(3), 1166–1183.

IND_IMG_CON.definition: The difference in luminance or color that makes an object distinguishable, measured via Root Mean Square Contrast.
IND_IMG_CON.formula: (1/3) * Sum_{b=1..3} sqrt( (1/(M*N)) * Sum_{i,j} (I_bij - I_b_bar)^2 ) where I_b_bar = (1/(M*N)) * Sum I_bij

variables.I_bij: Pixel intensity at row i, column j of color band b

IND_SVF_DEC.name: Sky View Factor Decrease

supporting_papers.citation: Li, X., Ratti, C., & Seiferling, I. (2018). Quantifying the shade provision of street trees in urban landscape: A case study in Boston, USA, using Google Street View. Landscape and Urban Planning, 169, 81–91.

IND_SVF_DEC.definition: The reduction in Sky View Factor attributed to street trees, defined as the difference between the simulation-based building-only SVF (SVFS, computed from a building-height/DSM model) and the photographic SVF (SVFP, computed from segmented GSV fisheye images that include both buildings and trees as sky obstructions). SVFdiff isolates the shading contribution of tree canopies.

variables.SVFS: Simulation-based, building-only Sky View Factor (computed from a building-height/DSM model; trees not represented)
variables.SVFP: Photographic, all-inclusive Sky View Factor (computed from segmented GSV-derived fisheye images; both buildings and trees obstruct sky)
variables.SVFdiff: SVF reduction attributable to street trees (>=0 when trees obstruct sky beyond buildings)

IND_SVF_DEC.first_confirmed_by: Li, X., Ratti, C., & Seiferling, I. (2018). Quantifying the shade provision of street trees in urban landscape: A case study in Boston, USA, using Google Street View. Landscape and Urban Planning, 169, 81-91.

IND_BEA_VIS.note_on_generality: Formula contains paper-specific regression coefficients (1.04165, 2.00634, 0.49522, 0.23200) fit on the source authors' dataset. These coefficients are NOT transferable across different cities/datasets without re-fitting. To compute IND_BEA_VIS in a new context, either (a) re-fit the regression with the same WI/C/PR predictors and a labeled beauty score sample, or (b) treat these as a literature reference and use a context-specific model.

supporting_papers.citation: Meng, Y., Shi, J., Lyu, M., Sun, D., & Fukuda, H. (2024). Research into the Influence Mechanisms of Visual-Comfort and Landscape Indicators of Urban Green Spaces. Land, 13(10), 1688.

Meng, Y., Sun, D., Lyu, M., Niu, J., & Fukuda, H. (2024). Measuring human perception of residential built environment through street view image and deep learning. Environmental Research Communications, 6(5), 055020.

IND_NAT_LND.definition: Naturalness (Ni): the ratio of the sum of percentages of natural-element pixels (trees, grass, water, mountains, plants) to the sum of percentages of artificial-element pixels (buildings, walls, roads, pavement, fences) across n street-view images at a sampling location.
IND_NAT_LND.formula: Ni = [(1/n) sum_{i=1..n} (Tn + Gn + W1n + M1n + P1n)] / [(1/n) sum_{i=1..n} (Bn + W2n + Rn + P2n + Fn)]

variables.n: number of images at the sampling location

IND_VSD.definition: Vegetation Structural Diversity (VSD): a Shannon-Wiener-based diversity index of the relative pixel proportions of vegetation strata (trees / shrubs / lawns) extracted from semantic segmentation of street view images, measuring vertical structural richness of street greenery (Tong et al. 2020).
IND_VSD.formula: VSD = - Sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i * ln(p_i), where p_i = C_i / C_veg

variables.p_i: Proportion of i-th vegetation type pixels relative to total vegetation pixels (C_i / C_veg)

variables.C_veg: Total pixel count across all vegetation types in the SVI
variables.N: Number of vegetation types present (0, 1, 2, or 3)

IND_VSG_BLK.definition: Block-level visible street greenery: a length- and distance-weighted aggregate of street-level Green View Index values from streets surrounding (within a buffer of) the block, with a power-law distance decay applied (per Ye, Xie, Fang, Jiang & Wang 2019, Sustainability — 'Daily Accessed Street Greenery and Housing Price').
IND_VSG_BLK.formula: G_n = Σ_i ( G_i · L_i · D_i^α ) / Σ_i ( L_i · D_i^α ) .

variables.G_n: Visible-greenery value aggregated to the n-th block
variables.G_i: Visible-greenery (GVI) value of the i-th surrounding street segment
variables.L_i: Length of the i-th surrounding street centerline
variables.D_i: Distance from the i-th street segment to the block (centroid or boundary)
variables.alpha: Distance-decay exponent (negative real number; sign convention to be confirmed against Ye et al. 2019)

IND_CPR.name: Compression Ratio

supporting_papers.citation: Zhao, J.; Suo, W. Research on the Construction and Application of a SVM-Based Quantification Model for Streetscape Visual Complexity. Land 2024, 13, 1953.

IND_CPR.definition: Compression Ratio is the ratio of the JPEG-compressed file size of a street-view image to its original (uncompressed) file size, used as a low-level proxy for streetscape visual complexity / information density. Per Zhao & Suo (2024), simple images contain more redundant, predictable information that JPEG can exploit, so they compress more effectively; complex scenes resist compression and therefore retain more information after compression. The paper formally defines CR = Size_compressed / Size_original.

variables.Size_compressed: file size (bytes) of the JPEG-compressed street-view image
variables.Size_original: file size (bytes) of the original uncompressed street-view image (e.g., BMP/RAW or pre-JPEG raster)

IND_CPR.first_confirmed_by: Zhao, J.; Suo, W. Research on the Construction and Application of a SVM-Based Quantification Model for Streetscape Visual Complexity. Land 2024, 13, 1953.

IND_GRC_CON.name: Grayscale Contrast (GLCM)

supporting_papers.citation: Zhao, J.; Suo, W. Research on the Construction and Application of a SVM-Based Quantification Model for Streetscape Visual Complexity. Land 2024, 13, 1953.

IND_GRC_CON.definition: Grayscale Contrast (GLCM) is a texture-based image-complexity metric computed from the Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) of a streetscape image. It captures local variations in grayscale intensity by summing the squared intensity differences of neighboring pixel pairs weighted by their co-occurrence probability. In Zhao & Suo (2024), the metric is computed in Python (skimage) by building GLCMs at multiple directions (0, 45, 90, 135 deg) and averaging their contrast values to obtain the overall grayscale contrast of the image; higher values indicate sharper edges, stronger black/white polarization, and richer texture detail.
IND_GRC_CON.formula: Contrast = Sum_{i=0}^{N-1} Sum_{j=0}^{N-1} (i - j)^2 * P(i, j)

variables.i: grayscale value (intensity level) of the first (reference) pixel; also the row index of the GLCM
variables.j: grayscale value (intensity level) of the adjacent (neighbor) pixel; also the column index of the GLCM
variables.P(i,j): normalized GLCM element giving the probability that a pixel with grayscale value i co-occurs with a pixel of grayscale value j at a specified direction and distance
variables.N: number of discrete grayscale levels (size of the GLCM)

IND_GRC_CON.formula_source: Zhao, J.; Suo, W. Research on the Construction and Application of a SVM-Based Quantification Model for Streetscape Visual Complexity. Land 2024, 13, 1953.

supporting_papers.citation: Jin, K., Yang, T., & Guo, W. (2023). Urban Street Quality Measurement Methods Combining Street View Images. 2023 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Intelligent Information Processing (AIIIP), 29–38.

IND_SQI.definition: A composite index measuring the overall spatial quality of an urban street based on visual indicators including green view, enclosure, motorization, and openness, derived via AHP weights.

variables.VEI: Visual Entropy Index (orderliness of grey-level distribution)

IND_SQI.first_confirmed_by: Jin, K., Guo, W., & Yang, T. (2023). Urban Street Quality Measurement Methods Combining Street View Images. 2023 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Intelligent Information Processing (AIIIP), 29-38.

IND_SQI.note_on_generality: Composite Street Quality Index using paper-specific regression coefficients (1.507, 0.692, 0.447, ..., 7.798, -6.344). These weights are derived from the source authors' specific dataset and city, not universal. To use IND_SQI in a new context, either (a) re-fit weights using the same 12 component indicators with a comparable training sample, or (b) treat as a literature benchmark and develop a context-specific composite.

IND_DIV_SIMP.name: Simpson Diversity Index
IND_DIV_SIMP.definition: Simpson visual diversity D = 1 - Sum n_i(n_i-1)/(N(N-1)) computed over a fixed set of 5 segmentation categories (excluding sky) aggregated either per-image or across multiple images of a neighborhood.

variables.n_i: Total pixel count in category i (summed across the k images of a neighborhood, i in 1..5; sky excluded)
variables.N: Sum over all categories: N = Sum_i n_i
variables.k: Number of images aggregated for the neighborhood
variables.categories: 5 selected segmentation categories (excludes sky to avoid dominance)

IND_VIB_GRN.name: Vibrant Green Space
IND_VIB_GRN.definition: Pixel ratio of street-view greenery that also passes a Hue-Saturation-Value (HSV) color filter for 'healthy' green hues. Refines the standard GVI by retaining only pixels that look both vegetative AND vividly green, producing a perception-relevant subset of GVI.
IND_VIB_GRN.formula: VibrantGreen = N(pixels labelled vegetation AND HSV in healthy-green range) / N(total pixels)
IND_VIB_GRN.variables: N(.) = pixel count; semantic class 'vegetation' from PSPNet; HSV range thresholds for healthy green hues

IND_CRI.name: Color Richness Index of Vegetation
IND_CRI.definition: Improvement on visual entropy by weighting each segmented vegetation region's entropy contribution by a Hasler-Susstrunk colorfulness factor (sigma_rgyb + 0.3 * mu_rgyb). Blends information richness with colorfulness over plant communities.
IND_CRI.formula: CR = sum_{i=1..n} M * P_i * log(P_i) where M = sigma_rgyb + 0.3 * mu_rgyb; rg = R - G; yb = 0.5*(R + G) - B; sigma_rgyb = sqrt(sigma_rg^2 + sigma_yb^2); mu_rgyb = sqrt(mu_rg^2 + mu_yb^2)
IND_CRI.variables: P_i = pixel proportion of the i-th vegetation region in the segmentation; M = colorfulness factor combining standard deviation and mean of rg / yb opponent channels

supporting_papers.citation: Qin, X., Yang, D., & Wangari, V. W. (2024). Quantitative characterization and evaluation of highway greening landscape spatial quality based on deep learning. Environmental Impact Assessment Review, 107, 107559.

IND_EXG.name: Excess Green Index (Pixel-Color Vegetation Index)
IND_EXG.definition: A pixel-level RGB-derived index that enhances green vegetation contrast in street-view images. Computed for every pixel from the normalized RGB bands; image-level EXG values can be averaged or thresholded (e.g. via Otsu) to extract vegetation without semantic segmentation.
IND_EXG.formula: ExG = 2*G - R - B ; per-image vegetation visibility = #{pixels: ExG > Otsu(ExG)} / Pixels_total
IND_EXG.variables: G, R, B = normalized green/red/blue band values of a pixel after segmentation-mean normalization (range 0-1); Otsu(ExG) = automatically chosen threshold per image.

supporting_papers.citation: Li, X., Zhang, C., & Li, W. (2015). Does the Visibility of Greenery Increase Perceived Safety in Urban Areas? Evidence from the Place Pulse 1.0 Dataset. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 4(3), 1166–1183.

IND_NVGI.name: Normalized Vegetation Greenery Index (RGB-based)
IND_NVGI.definition: A normalized RGB-based pixel index designed to substitute for satellite NDVI when only RGB street-view data is available. Higher NVGI separates green vegetation from other features in BSV/GSV imagery.
IND_NVGI.formula: NVGI = G0 / sqrt(R0 * B0)  where  R0 = (R_pixel - R_min)/(R_max - R_min), G0 = (G_pixel - G_min)/(G_max - G_min), B0 = (B_pixel - B_min)/(B_max - B_min)
IND_NVGI.variables: R_pixel, G_pixel, B_pixel = raw RGB values of a pixel; R_min/max, G_min/max, B_min/max = per-image min/max of each channel; R0, G0, B0 = min-max normalized channel values.

supporting_papers.citation: Song, J., Zhu, X., & Yu, X. (2023). Monitoring and mapping vegetation greenery in residential communities using street view images and a Normalized Vegetation Greenery Index: a case study in Beijing, China. Frontiers in Forests and Global Change, 6, 1071569.

IND_GVI_HOR_ABV.name: Above-Horizon Green View Index
IND_GVI_HOR_ABV.definition: The percentage of green-vegetation pixels located above the camera 2.5 m horizon line (i.e. vegetation higher than the camera height of ~2.5 m) within a street-view image. It captures non-view-obstructing tree-canopy greenery and is computed by splitting the image at a horizon row that depends on camera pitch. (Li et al. 2015 ISPRS IJGI Place Pulse 1.0 protocol)
IND_GVI_HOR_ABV.formula: GVI_above = N(green_pixels with row < horizon_row) / N(total_pixels);    horizon_row = (H_img / 2) - f_px * tan(pitch_rad);    Place Pulse 1.0 specific: horizon_row = 150 - 200 * tan(pitch) for 400x30

variables.green_pixels: pixels classified as vegetation by semantic segmentation
variables.total_pixels: total pixel count of the image (= W * H)
variables.H_img: image height in pixels (300 for Place Pulse 1.0 GSV; 'H' was used in Li 2015 as the per-pixel horizon offset, not image height)
variables.W_img: image width in pixels (400 for Place Pulse 1.0 GSV)
variables.f_px: camera focal length in pixels; for Place Pulse 1.0 GSV (400x300, HFOV 90 deg) f_px = 200; in general f_px = (W_img/2) / tan(HFOV_rad/2)
variables.pitch_rad: camera pitch angle in radians; positive = camera tilts up (horizon line moves down in image); 0 = level camera
variables.horizon_row: pixel row corresponding to the 2.5 m physical horizon line in the image; reduces to H_img/2 for level (pitch = 0) cameras

supporting_papers.citation: Li, X., Zhang, C., & Li, W. (2015). Does the Visibility of Greenery Increase Perceived Safety in Urban Areas? Evidence from the Place Pulse 1.0 Dataset. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 4(3), 1166–1183.

IND_GVI_HOR_ABV.first_confirmed_by: Li, X., Zhang, C., & Li, W. (2015). Does the Visibility of Greenery Increase Perceived Safety in Urban Areas? Evidence from the Place Pulse 1.0 Dataset. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 4(3), 1166–1183.

IND_GVI_HOR_ABV.input_data_requirements: RGB + semantic segmentation + horizon detection (simple geometry or fixed horizon-row setting)

parameters_required.f_px: focal length in pixels (camera dependent; default H/(2·tan(FOV/2)) if FOV known)
parameters_required.pitch_rad: camera pitch (typically 0 for level GSV captures)

IND_GVI_HOR_ABV.note_on_generality: Original Li et al. (2015) hardcoded H=300, f=200 px for their 400x300 GSV imagery. Generalized formula here parameterizes H, f_px, and pitch so that the indicator works on any streetview image size and camera. For panoramic-equirectangular images, replace horizon_row by H/2 (zero-pitch assumption).

IND_GVI_HOR_BLW.name: Below-Horizon Green View Index
IND_GVI_HOR_BLW.definition: Percentage of green-vegetation pixels located below the camera's 2.5 m horizon line in a GSV image — these pixels correspond to vegetation lower than 2.5 m (lawns, shrubs, small trees), which Li et al. 2015 treat as the potentially view-obstructing component of street greenery.
IND_GVI_HOR_BLW.formula: GVI_below = N(green pixels with row >= horizon_row) / N(total pixels); horizon_row = (H_image / 2) + f_px · tan(pitch_rad)  [Li et al. 2015, Eq. 4: Horizon = 150 + 200·tan(pitch) for 400×300 GSV image

variables.green_pixels: pixels classified as vegetation by the segmentation method
variables.total_pixels: total pixel count of the image (= W * H_image)
variables.H_image: image height in pixels (300 in Place Pulse 1.0)
variables.W: image width in pixels (400 in Place Pulse 1.0)
variables.f_px: camera focal length in pixels (200 for the Place Pulse 1.0 dataset)
variables.pitch_rad: camera pitch angle in radians (signed; positive = camera tilted up)
variables.horizon_row: pixel row index of the 2.5 m physical horizon line in the image

supporting_papers.citation: Li, X., Zhang, C., & Li, W. (2015). Does the Visibility of Greenery Increase Perceived Safety in Urban Areas? Evidence from the Place Pulse 1.0 Dataset. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 4(3), 1166–1183.

IND_GVI_HOR_BLW.first_confirmed_by: Li, X., Zhang, C., & Li, W. (2015). Does the Visibility of Greenery Increase Perceived Safety in Urban Areas? Evidence from the Place Pulse 1.0 Dataset. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 4(3), 1166–1183.

IND_NAT_ART.name: Natural-to-Artificial Element Ratio
IND_NAT_ART.definition: Ratio of the average proportion of natural-element pixels (trees, grass, water, mountains, plants) to the average proportion of artificial-element pixels (buildings, walls, roads, pavement, fences) across all SVIs at a sample location. Captures how nature-dominated the visible streetscape is relative to its built fabric.
IND_NAT_ART.formula: Ni = [ (1/n) Sum(Tn) + (1/n) Sum(Gn) + (1/n) Sum(W1n) + (1/n) Sum(M1n) + (1/n) Sum(P1n) ] / [ (1/n) Sum(Bn) + (1/n) Sum(W2n) + (1/n) Sum(Rn) + (1/n) Sum(P2n) + (1/n) Sum(Fn) ]
IND_NAT_ART.variables: Tn = % tree pixels; Gn = % grass pixels; W1n = % water pixels; M1n = % mountain pixels; P1n = % plant pixels; Bn = % building pixels; W2n = % wall pixels; Rn = % road pixels; P2n = % pavement pixels; Fn = % fence pixels; n = number of images at the site

IND_ENC_RATIO.name: Visual Enclosure Ratio (Vertical-to-Horizontal)
IND_ENC_RATIO.definition: Ratio of mean enclosing-element pixel proportions (buildings + trees + walls) to mean opening/ground-element pixel proportions (pavement + fences + roads) across n SVIs sampled at a site. Higher values indicate stronger vertical-interface enclosure of the street/space. (Meng et al. 2024, Land, Table 1)

variables.T_n: percentage of tree pixels in image n

variables.P1_n: percentage of pavement pixels in image n
variables.F_n: percentage of fence pixels in image n
variables.R_n: percentage of road pixels in image n
variables.n: number of panoramic SVIs sampled at a single site (Meng 2024 used n images per sample point)

IND_OVH_SHL.name: Overhead Shelter Ratio
IND_OVH_SHL.definition: Pixel-area proportion of overhead-sheltering elements (trees, ceiling, canopy) in the TOP VIEW of a panorama (i.e. the bird's-eye sub-image of a 360° GSV panorama, not the upright street-view image); operationalizes the PSD 'sheltered' dimension as a feeling of safety and shelter.

variables.Pixels_tree: Pixels classified as 'tree' by PSPNet (ADE20K) within the top-view sub-image of the panorama
variables.Pixels_ceiling: Pixels classified as 'ceiling' within the top-view sub-image
variables.Pixels_canopy: Pixels classified as 'canopy' (e.g. rain shelter) within the top-view sub-image
variables.Pixels_total_topview: Total pixel count of the top-view sub-image of the panorama

IND_MTD.name: Motorization Degree
IND_MTD.definition: Sum of pixel proportions of all motor-traffic-related elements (road surface plus all vehicle classes plus railway) in a street view image. Captures how dominant motor traffic infrastructure and vehicles are in the visual scene, distinct from a single vehicle ratio.
IND_MTD.formula: MD = (P_road + P_car + P_bus + P_truck + P_freight_vehicle + P_small_motor_vehicle + P_railway) / P_all
IND_MTD.variables: P_road, P_car, P_bus, P_truck, P_freight_vehicle, P_small_motor_vehicle, P_railway = pixel counts of each class; P_all = total pixels

IND_IMG.name: Imageability (Streetscape)
IND_IMG.definition: Average pixel proportion across n images of building, traffic-light, and traffic-sign elements at a sampling point. Operationalises Lynch's 'imageability' from street view images as the prevalence of distinctive landmark and signage elements.
IND_IMG.formula: I_k = (1/n) * sum_{j=1..n} B_jk + (1/n) * sum_{j=1..n} TL_jk + (1/n) * sum_{j=1..n} TS_jk
IND_IMG.variables: n = number of images at sampling point k; B_jk = % building pixels in image j; TL_jk = % traffic-light pixels; TS_jk = % traffic-sign pixels

IND_IRI.definition: Number of distinct semantic categories detected in a street-view image divided by the total number of categories supported by the segmentation model; measures categorical richness of the streetscape interface.

IND_DIV_MCINTOSH.name: McIntosh Visual Diversity Index
IND_DIV_MCINTOSH.definition: A multi-category diversity index applied to the pixel counts of five 'permanent' semantic-segmentation classes (greenery, building, sky/openness, road, sidewalk) aggregated over all k street-view images of a spatial unit; ranges from 0 (no diversity) to 1 (extreme diversity).

variables.n_i: Pixel count of category i (i in [1,5]) summed over all k images, n_i = sum_{k} c_ki
variables.c_ki: Pixel count of category i in image k
variables.N: Grand total sum_{i=1..5} n_i (over the five permanent categories)
variables.k: Index over street-view images in the spatial unit (block / neighbourhood / city)

IND_FG_ERI.name: Foreground Edge Roughness Index (S_ERI)
IND_FG_ERI.definition: A shape metric describing the irregularity of the foreground outline derived from semantic-segmented street/scenic images, based on the isoperimetric inequality. Compares the perimeter of the foreground region to the square root of its area; values near sqrt(2*pi) indicate circular outlines, values near 1 indicate square outlines, and larger values indicate increasingly irregular shapes.

variables.P: number of pixels on the foreground outline (perimeter)
variables.A: number of pixels contained within the foreground (area)

IND_FG_ECI.name: Foreground Edge Contrast Index (S_ECI)
IND_FG_ECI.definition: Measures how distinct the foreground is from its surrounding elements by checking element-label consistency of the 8-neighborhood for each pixel on the foreground outline. Values near 0 indicate the foreground is sharply distinguished from its surroundings; values near 1 indicate it blends with neighbors.

variables.Py: number of foreground-outline pixels whose 8 neighbors share the same element label (marked yellow)
variables.Pb: number of foreground-outline pixels whose 8 neighbors have differing element labels (marked blue)

IND_FG_PSI.name: Foreground Hole-Shape Similarity Index (S_PSI)
IND_FG_PSI.definition: A distributional trend metric describing how similar in shape the multiple holes (open patches inside the foreground) are. Computed as the coefficient of variation of the perimeter-area shape descriptor (0.25*Pi/sqrt(Ai)) across all N holes. Zero means all holes are similar in shape; larger values mean increasingly dissimilar hole shapes.
IND_FG_PSI.formula: S_PSI = sqrt( (1/N) * sum_i ( (0.25*Pi/sqrt(Ai)) - mu )^2 ) / mu , where mu = (1/N) sum_i (0.25*Pi/sqrt(Ai))

variables.Pi: perimeter pixels of the i-th hole inside the foreground
variables.Ai: area pixels of the i-th hole inside the foreground
variables.N: number of holes inside the foreground
variables.mu: mean of the per-hole shape ratios

supporting_papers.citation: Lan, J., Liu, M., Luiten, E., Bracken, G., & Zhang, Q. (2025). Exploring spatial visual characteristics of scenic archetypes through AI multimodal mapping methods in Hangzhou Westlake. npj Heritage Science, 13, 658.

IND_FG_API.name: Foreground Asymmetry / Profile Index (S_API)
IND_FG_API.definition: A distributional-trend metric describing the lateral asymmetry of the foreground outline relative to the vertical midline of the field of view, computed as the standard deviation of the per-row midpoints of the foreground outline's left/right pixel pairs about the field-of-view midline. Higher values indicate stronger left/right asymmetry of the foreground silhouette. (Lan et al. 2025, npj Heritage Science, Table 1)

variables.P_i: horizontal coordinate of the midpoint of the i-th row pixel pair on the two sides of the foreground outline, P_i = (x_{i,left} + x_{i,right}) / 2
variables.x_{i,left}: horizontal coordinate of the leftmost foreground-outline pixel on row i
variables.x_{i,right}: horizontal coordinate of the rightmost foreground-outline pixel on row i
variables.mu: horizontal coordinate of the vertical midline of the field of view (= W/2 for an image of width W)
variables.N: number of image rows that contain a left/right outline pixel pair (i.e. number of P_i values)

supporting_papers.citation: Lan, J., Liu, M., Luiten, E., Bracken, G., & Zhang, Q. (2025). Exploring spatial visual characteristics of scenic archetypes through AI multimodal mapping methods in Hangzhou Westlake. npj Heritage Science, 13, 658.

IND_FG_ADI.name: Foreground Largest-Hole Asymmetry Index (S_ADI)
IND_FG_ADI.definition: A relative-relationship metric capturing whether one hole inside the foreground dominates the others in size. Equal to (SDi - SDj)/SDi, where SDi is the standard deviation of all hole areas including the largest one and SDj is the standard deviation excluding the largest. Zero indicates the largest hole is similar in size to the rest; one indicates the largest hole dominates.
IND_FG_ADI.formula: S_ADI = (SDi - SDj) / SDi ; SDi = sqrt((1/N)*sum_i(Ai - mu_i)^2) ; SDj = sqrt((1/N)*sum_{i!=max}(Ai - mu_w)^2)

variables.Ai: number of pixels contained in the i-th hole in the foreground
variables.mu_w: mean of Ai with the largest Ai removed
variables.SDi: standard deviation of all hole areas
variables.SDj: standard deviation of hole areas excluding the largest one

supporting_papers.citation: Lan, J., Liu, M., Luiten, E., Bracken, G., & Zhang, Q. (2025). Exploring spatial visual characteristics of scenic archetypes through AI multimodal mapping methods in Hangzhou Westlake. npj Heritage Science, 13, 658.

IND_GLCM_ENT.name: Texture Entropy (GLCM Entropy)
IND_GLCM_ENT.definition: Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix entropy applied to a street-view panorama, measuring textural non-uniformity / complexity of recurring local visual patterns.
IND_GLCM_ENT.formula: vis_texture = - Σ_{i=0..255} Σ_{j=0..255} pg_{ij} * log2 C_{ij} where C_{ij} is the normalized GLCM entry at offset (Δx, Δy)
IND_GLCM_ENT.variables: C_{ij} = normalized count in the GLCM at grayscale pair (i, j); pg_{ij} = associated probability of that grayscale pair.

IND_GVI_LATBAND.name: Green Visibility by Latitude Band (Zenith-Angle Banded GVI)
IND_GVI_LATBAND.definition: Green visibility computed inside each of FOUR latitude (zenith-angle) bands (0-22.5 deg, 22.5-45.0 deg, 45.0-67.5 deg, 67.5-90.0 deg) on a sky-map (orthographic-projection fisheye) image obtained by transforming an equi-cylindrical GSV panorama; expresses how vegetation visibility varies with viewing elevation.
IND_GVI_LATBAND.formula: GV_band(k) = N(vegetation pixels in latitude band k) / N(total sky-map pixels in latitude band k), for k in {(0,22.5], (22.5,45], (45,67.5], (67.5,90]}, k = 1..4

variables.GV_band(k): Green visibility within latitude band k of the sky-map
variables.k: Latitude band index, k = 1..4 (Sakamoto et al. 2023 use exactly four bands)
variables.vegetation_pixels: Pixels labeled as the 'vegetation' class (Sakamoto used a single vegetation aggregate class on a SegNet-style model trained on Cityscapes-like labels)
variables.total_pixels: Total sky-map pixels falling inside band k (excluding non-sky-map area outside the orthographic disc)

International Journal of Health Geographics, 22, 29.

IND_GVI_LATBAND.input_data_requirements: Panoramic image (spherical / fisheye projection) + banding by zenith angle + semantic segmentation
IND_GVI_LATBAND.note_on_generality: Sakamoto et al. (2023) used 5 latitude bands and Cityscapes-trained segmentation. Generalized to any number of bands and any urban segmentation labelset.
IND_GVI_LATBAND.note_on_band_choice: Sakamoto et al. (2023) used 5 bands at boundaries (0, 22.5, 45, 67.5, 90)°. The number of bands and their boundaries should be set based on the analytical question (e.g., distinguishing low ground vegetation from high tree canopies). Common choices: 5 bands as in Sakamoto, or 3 bands (lower/middle/upper hemisphere).

IND_VGV.name: Visual Greenery Vector / Field (VGF)
IND_VGV.definition: A 2D vector at each sampling point whose components are signed sums of directional GVI values (north-south on Y; east-west on X), so that the magnitude expresses the strength and the orientation expresses the direction of maximum visible greenery (after Stancato 2024). The collection of such vectors over a region constitutes the Visual Greenery Field (VGF).
IND_VGV.formula: v_x = GVI_E - GVI_W ; v_y = GVI_N - GVI_S ; |v| = sqrt(v_x^2 + v_y^2) ; theta = atan2(v_y, v_x)  [theta is implicit in the paper but standard]

variables.GVI_N, GVI_S, GVI_E, GVI_W: GVI computed from each of four 90-deg-FOV cardinal-direction images at the same location; vegetation class is the ADE20K aggregate {tree, plant, palm, grass, flower, field}
variables.v_x, v_y: X (east-west) and Y (north-south) components of the synthetic greenery vector
variables.|v|: Magnitude (intensity) of the visual greenery vector
variables.theta: Direction (angle from +X / east axis) of the visual greenery vector — derived as atan2(v_y, v_x); paper gives the vector but does not write theta explicitly

supporting_papers.citation: Stancato, G. (2024). The Visual Greenery Field: Representing the Urban Green Visual Continuum with Street View Image Analysis. Sustainability, 16(21), 9512.

IND_VGV.first_confirmed_by: Stancato, G. (2024). The Visual Greenery Field: Representing the Urban Green Visual Continuum with Street View Image Analysis. Sustainability, 16(21), 9512.

IND_DPT_MEAN.name: Average Scene Depth
IND_DPT_MEAN.definition: Per-image average of pixel-wise scene depth values estimated by a monocular depth-estimation network (e.g., DPT-Large, MiDaS) applied to a street-view image; reflects the mean perceived distance to objects in the visible scene.
IND_DPT_MEAN.formula: Depth_mean = (1 / (M*N)) * sum_{i,j} D(i,j), where D(i,j) is the predicted depth value at pixel (i,j) of an MxN image
IND_DPT_MEAN.variables: D(i,j) = predicted scene-depth value at pixel (i,j), produced by a pretrained depth-estimation model (DPT-Large in Ma et al. 2023); M, N = image dimensions

supporting_papers.citation: Ma, H., Xu, Q., & Zhang, Y. (2023). High or low? Exploring the restorative effects of visual levels on campus spaces using machine learning and street view imagery. Urban Forestry & Urban Greening, 88, 128087.

IND_FG_DEPTH_LV.name: Foreground Maximum Depth Value (P_LV)
IND_FG_DEPTH_LV.definition: An absolute-value position metric: the maximum depth value across all pixels in the foreground, derived from monocular depth estimation grayscale (1-256). High values mean the foreground starts close to the observer; low values mean the foreground starts far from the observer.

variables.Di: depth value (1-256 grayscale) of the i-th pixel in the foreground

supporting_papers.citation: Lan, J., Liu, M., Luiten, E., Bracken, G., & Zhang, Q. (2025). Exploring spatial visual characteristics of scenic archetypes through AI multimodal mapping methods in Hangzhou Westlake. npj Heritage Science, 13, 658.

IND_FG_DEPTH_LV.input_data_requirements: Depth map + foreground semantic mask (take the maximum depth value over the foreground region)

IND_FG_DEPTH_LN.name: Foreground Vertical Depth Length (P_LN)
IND_FG_DEPTH_LN.definition: An absolute-value position metric: the count of unique non-repeating depth values among pixels in the foreground, representing the vertical depth extent (range) of the foreground.

variables.Di: depth value of the i-th pixel in the foreground (grayscale 1-256)
variables.Unique(Di): indicator that returns 1 only if Di is a non-repeating depth value within the foreground
variables.N: total number of pixels in the foreground

supporting_papers.citation: Lan, J., Liu, M., Luiten, E., Bracken, G., & Zhang, Q. (2025). Exploring spatial visual characteristics of scenic archetypes through AI multimodal mapping methods in Hangzhou Westlake. npj Heritage Science, 13, 658.

IND_FG_DEPTH_ECI.name: Foreground Depth Continuity Index (P_ECI)
IND_FG_DEPTH_ECI.definition: A distributional trend metric measuring how smoothly depth values transition across the foreground. Computed as the coefficient of variation of the per-row/column coefficients of variation of the absolute differences between depth values of neighboring-pixel pairs. Zero means depth values are continuously consistent; larger values mean abrupt depth jumps.
IND_FG_DEPTH_ECI.formula: P_ECI = sqrt( (1/N) * sum_j (CV_j - mu_CV)^2 ) / mu_CV ; CV_j = sqrt((1/Mj)*sum_i(Cij - mu_j)^2) / mu_j ; Cij = |idx_i - idx_{i+1}|

variables.Cij: absolute difference in depth values between the i-th pair of neighboring pixels in the j-th row or column
variables.mu_j: mean of all Cij in the j-th row or column
variables.CV_j: coefficient of variation of all Cij in the j-th row or column
variables.mu_CV: average of CV_j across all rows and columns
variables.Mj: number of Cij in the j-th row or column
variables.N: total number of rows and columns in the foreground

CAT_MTH.name: Methods
CAT_MTH.definition: Root category for analytical, computational, and procedural methods used in SVF/streetview studies (e.g., simulation, projection, regression-based analyses).

CAT_TOL.name: Tools
CAT_TOL.definition: Root category for software, libraries, frameworks, and computing platforms used in SVF/streetview analyses (e.g., deep-learning toolkits, segmentation packages, GIS software).

CAT_THR.name: Theories
CAT_THR.definition: Root category for theoretical frameworks and conceptual models cited as motivating or interpretive lenses in SVF/streetview research (e.g., Broken Windows Theory, Perceived Sensory Dimensions).

CAT_REL.name: Relationships
CAT_REL.definition: Root category for inter-variable relationship types modeled in SVF/streetview studies (e.g., mediation, moderation, threshold, weighting).

CAT_DES.name: Study Design
CAT_DES.definition: Root category for high-level research designs used in SVF/streetview studies (e.g., literature review, cross-sectional, longitudinal, experimental).

CAT_SPU.name: Sample Units
CAT_SPU.definition: Root category for the spatial/observational units at which streetview imagery and outcomes are sampled (e.g., trajectory, street block, point, segment).

CAT_SET.name: Settings
CAT_SET.definition: Root category for the physical/urban settings in which SVF/streetview studies are conducted (e.g., CBD, greenway, metro station area, historic district, industrial area, coastal street).

CAT_CNT.name: Countries
CAT_CNT.definition: Root category for countries (and special regions) where SVF/streetview studies were conducted; children are ISO-style country codes (CNT_*).

CAT_OST.name: Object Subtypes
CAT_OST.definition: Root category for fine-grained subtypes of detected streetview objects (e.g., tall tree, fence, sign), nested under broader OBJ_* object classes.

CAT_CTX.name: Context Classifications
CAT_CTX.definition: Root category for context classifications applied to study sites, including Local Climate Zone (LCZ) typologies and other urban-form context schemes used to stratify streetview observations.

CAT_TST.name: Statistical Tests (root)
CAT_TST.definition: Root category for statistical-test codes (D_stat_tests section).

MTH_PWC.name: Pairwise Comparison
MTH_PWC.definition: MTH_PWC is a comparative evaluation method where items or criteria are compared against each other in pairs to establish a hierarchy or relative weighting. Often associated with the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), this approach reduces the cognitive complexity of judging many items simultaneously. By focusing on the preference or importance of one element over another in isolation (e.g., 'Is Scene A more pleasant than Scene B?'), this method generates robust, consistent data for ranking landscape qualities or subjective preferences.

supporting_papers.citation: Zhao, X., Lu, Y., & Lin, G. (2024). An integrated deep learning approach for assessing the visual qualities of built environments utilizing street view images. Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 130, 107805.

MTH_PWC.first_confirmed_by: Zhao, X., & Lin, G. (2024). Research on the Perception Evaluation of Urban Green Spaces Using Panoramic Images and Deep Learning: A Case Study of Zhujiang Park in Guangzhou. Landscape Architecture Frontiers, 12(6), 007-024.

MTH_QST.name: Questionnaire
MTH_QST.definition: MTH_QST is a structured data collection instrument consisting of a series of questions or prompts designed to gather information from respondents. In environmental perception studies, questionnaires typically utilize Likert scales or semantic differentials to capture subjective data regarding preferences, emotional responses, or demographic information. This method allows for the standardized quantification of qualitative human experiences, enabling statistical analysis of how different user groups perceive and value specific environmental attributes or urban design interventions.

supporting_papers.citation: Qiu, Y., Pan, H., Kalantari, Z., Giusti, M., & Che, S. (2023). The natural focus: Combining deep learning and eye-tracking to understand public perceptions of urban ecosystem aesthetics. Ecological Indicators, 156, 111181.

MTH_ISO.name: Isovist Analysis

supporting_papers.citation: Cai, M., Xiang, L., & Ng, E. (2023). How does the visual environment influence pedestrian physiological stress? Evidence from high-density cities using ambulatory technology and spatial machine learning. Sustainable Cities and Society, 96, 104695.

MTH_ISO.definition: A geometrical method for analyzing the visible area from a specific vantage point.
MTH_ISO.first_confirmed_by: Cai, M., Xiang, L., & Ng, E. (2023). How does the visual environment influence pedestrian physiological stress? Evidence from high-density cities using ambulatory technology and spatial machine learning. Sustainable Cities and Society, 96, 104695.

MTH_MAN.definition: Human visual identification and classification of image pixels.
MTH_MAN.first_confirmed_by: Chen, X., Meng, Q., Hu, D., Zhang, L., & Yang, J. (2019). Evaluating Greenery around Streets Using Baidu Panoramic Street View Images and the Panoramic Green View Index. Forests, 10(12), 1109.

MTH_FSH.name: Fisheye Projection Analysis

MTH_FSH.definition: A method of processing images to simulate or analyze a 180-degree hemispherical view, often used for calculating view factors.
MTH_FSH.first_confirmed_by: Hua, J., Cai, M., Shi, Y., Ren, C., Xie, J., Chung, L. C. H., ... & Webster, C. (2022). Investigating pedestrian-level greenery in urban forms in a high-density city for urban planning. Sustainable Cities and Society, 80, 103755.

MTH_PHO.name: Photographic Method

supporting_papers.citation: Li, X., Ratti, C., & Seiferling, I. (2018). Quantifying the shade provision of street trees in urban landscape: A case study in Boston, USA, using Google Street View. Landscape and Urban Planning, 169, 81–91.

MTH_PHO.definition: A method for calculating view factors (like SVF) by projecting a fisheye image onto a hemisphere and integrating the visible angles (Steyn, 1980).
MTH_PHO.first_confirmed_by: Li, X., Santi, P., Courtney, T. K., Verma, S. K., & Ratti, C. (2018). Investigating the association between streetscapes and human walking activities using Google Street View and human trajectory data. Transactions in GIS, 22(4).

MTH_RAY.name: Ray Tracing Analysis

supporting_papers.citation: Wu, R., Fang, X., Brown, R., Liu, S., & Zhao, H. (2023). Establishing a link between complex courtyard spaces and thermal comfort: A major advancement in evidence-based design. Building and Environment, 245, 110852.

MTH_RAY.definition: A computational method simulating light paths to calculate obstruction or visibility, often used with 3D building models to determine Sky View Factors.
MTH_RAY.first_confirmed_by: Li, X., Santi, P., Courtney, T. K., Verma, S. K., & Ratti, C. (2018). Investigating the association between streetscapes and human walking activities using Google Street View and human trajectory data. Transactions in GIS, 22(4).

MTH_SML.name: Simulation Modeling

supporting_papers.citation: Li, X., Ratti, C., & Seiferling, I. (2018). Quantifying the shade provision of street trees in urban landscape: A case study in Boston, USA, using Google Street View. Landscape and Urban Planning, 169, 81–91.

Zhu, H., Nan, X., Yang, F., & Bao, Z. (2023). Utilizing the green view index to improve the urban street greenery index system: A statistical study using road patterns and vegetation structures as entry points. Landscape and Urban Planning, 237, 104780.

MTH_SML.definition: The use of digital 3D models (building footprints, DSM, LiDAR) to computationally simulate environmental parameters (e.g., solar radiation, wind, SVF) without direct physical measurement.
MTH_SML.first_confirmed_by: Li, X., Ratti, C., & Seiferling, I. (2018). Quantifying the shade provision of street trees in urban landscape: A case study in Boston, USA, using Google Street View. Landscape and Urban Planning, 169, 81-91.

MTH_SPA_AUT.name: Spatial Autocorrelation Analysis

supporting_papers.citation: Xue, C., Jin, C., Zhou, L., & Li, G. (2022). Exploring the distribution of city street greenery from eye-level: An application of Baidu Map panoramic images data. Geografisk Tidsskrift-Danish Journal of Geography, 122(1), 73–86.

MTH_SPA_AUT.definition: A method to measure the correlation of a variable with itself through space (e.g., Moran's I).
MTH_SPA_AUT.first_confirmed_by: Long, Y., & Liu, L. (2017). How green are the streets? An analysis for central areas of Chinese cities using Tencent Street View. PLOS ONE, 12(2), e0171110.

MTH_UME.name: Urban Multi-scale Environmental Predictor

supporting_papers.citation: Ni, Y., Lin, L., Xia, H., & Wang, X. (2025). Urban Street Greening and Resident Comfort: An Integrated Approach Based on High-Precision Shadow Distribution and Facade Visual Assessment. Sustainability, 17(3), 1026.

MTH_UME.definition: A climate service tool (UMEP) used to model outdoor thermal comfort, shadow patterns, and solar radiation.
MTH_UME.first_confirmed_by: Ni, Y.; Lin, L.; Xia, H.; Wang, X. Urban Street Greening and Resident Comfort: An Integrated Approach Based on High-Precision Shadow Distribution and Facade Visual Assessment. Sustainability 2025, 17, 1026.

MTH_AHP.name: Analytic Hierarchy Process

supporting_papers.citation: Jin, K., Yang, T., & Guo, W. (2023). Urban Street Quality Measurement Methods Combining Street View Images. 2023 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Intelligent Information Processing (AIIIP), 29–38.

MTH_AHP.definition: A structured technique for organizing and analyzing complex decisions, based on mathematics and psychology, used here for weighting indicators.
MTH_AHP.first_confirmed_by: Ni, Y.; Lin, L.; Xia, H.; Wang, X. Urban Street Greening and Resident Comfort: An Integrated Approach Based on High-Precision Shadow Distribution and Facade Visual Assessment. Sustainability 2025, 17, 1026.

MTH_ORT_PRJ.name: Orthographic Projection Transformation

supporting_papers.citation: Sakamoto, S., Kogure, M., Hanibuchi, T., Nakaya, N., Hozawa, A., & Nakaya, T. (2023). Effects of greenery at different heights in neighbourhood streetscapes on leisure walking: a cross-sectional study using machine learning of streetscape images in Sendai City, Japan. International Journal of Health Geographics, 22, 29.

MTH_ORT_PRJ.definition: The transformation of equi-cylindrical panoramic images into an orthographic (fisheye/sky map) projection to analyze elements based on vertical angular position relative to the viewer.
MTH_ORT_PRJ.first_confirmed_by: Sakamoto, S., Kogure, M., Hanibuchi, T. et al. Effects of greenery at different heights in neighbourhood streetscapes on leisure walking: a cross-sectional study using machine learning of streetscape images in Sendai City, Japan. Int J Health Geogr 22, 29 (2023).

MTH_RNG_DIV.name: Vertical Viewing Angle Division

supporting_papers.citation: Tong, Z., & Gong, C. (2022). Integrating Characteristics of Vertical Viewing Angle into Quantitative Analysis of Street View Photos. 2022 29th International Conference on Geoinformatics, 1–6.

MTH_RNG_DIV.definition: The method of dividing a street view image into concentric rings (e.g., 0-30°, 30-50°, 50-90°) to analyze visual elements within specific fields of view.
MTH_RNG_DIV.first_confirmed_by: Tong, Z., & Gong, C. (2022). Integrating Characteristics of Vertical Viewing Angle into Quantitative Analysis of Street View Photos. 2022 29th International Conference on Geoinformatics, 1-6.

MTH_FSH_PRJ.name: Fisheye Projection (for SVF computation)

supporting_papers.citation: Zou, J., Jiang, H., Ying, W., & Qiu, B. (2024). Scenic Influences on Walking Preferences in Urban Forest Parks from Top-View and Eye-Level Perspectives. Forests, 15(11), 2020.

MTH_FSH_PRJ.definition: Geometric reprojection of panoramic or wide-FOV images to a fisheye view for hemispheric Sky View Factor calculation.
MTH_FSH_PRJ.first_confirmed_by: Xu, H.; Lu, H.; Liu, S. Online Street View-Based Approach for Sky View Factor Estimation: A Case Study of Nanjing, China. Appl. Sci. 2024, 14, 2133.

MTH_SBE.name: Scenic Beauty Estimation

supporting_papers.citation: Zhu, J., Li, B., Ouyang, H., Wang, Y., & Bai, Z. (2024). Understanding Urban Residents' Walking Exercise Preferences: An Empirical Study Using Street View Images and Trajectory Data. Buildings, 14(2), 549.

MTH_SBE.definition: A psychophysical method developed to assess the visual quality of a landscape through human ratings, often by experts or the public.
MTH_SBE.first_confirmed_by: Zhu, J.; Li, B.; Ouyang, H.; Wang, Y.; Bai, Z. Understanding Urban Residents’ Walking Exercise Preferences: An Empirical Study Using Street View Images and Trajectory Data. Buildings 2024, 14, 549.

MTH_CLS.name: Cluster Analysis
MTH_CLS.definition: A multivariate statistical technique and unsupervised machine learning method used to group a set of objects in such a way that objects in the same group (called a cluster) are more similar to each other—in terms of specific variables or dimensions—than to those in other groups. It is frequently used in urban studies to identify spatial patterns or typologies.

supporting_papers.citation: Torkko, J., Poom, A., Willberg, E., & Toivonen, T. (2023). How to best map greenery from a human perspective? Comparing computational measurements with human perception. Frontiers in Sustainable Cities, 5.

MTH_CLS.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_OIA.name: Object-Based Image Analysis (OBIA)
MTH_OIA.definition: A remote sensing and computer vision method that analyzes digital imagery by partitioning it into meaningful image objects (segments) based on spectral, geometrical, and topological characteristics, rather than analyzing individual pixels. This approach is particularly effective for high-resolution street view or satellite imagery to classify complex urban features.

supporting_papers.citation: Li, X., Zhang, C., & Li, W. (2015). Does the Visibility of Greenery Increase Perceived Safety in Urban Areas? Evidence from the Place Pulse 1.0 Dataset. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 4(3), 1166–1183.

MTH_OIA.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_TSV.name: Tencent Street View Analysis
MTH_TSV.definition: A methodological approach utilizing the application programming interfaces (APIs) and imagery datasets provided by Tencent Maps. Researchers employ this method to extract large-scale, street-level visual data for analyzing urban built environments, assessing greenery, calculating walkability, and modeling human-scale perceptions of city spaces in China.

supporting_papers.citation: Tong, M., She, J., Tan, J., Li, M., Ge, R., & Gao, Y. (2020). Evaluating Street Greenery by Multiple Indicators Using Street-Level Imagery and Satellite Images: A Case Study in Nanjing, China. Forests, 11(12), 1347.

MTH_TSV.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_HOT.name: Hot Spot Analysis
MTH_HOT.definition: A spatial analysis technique, often utilizing the Getis-Ord Gi* statistic, used to identify statistically significant spatial clusters of high values (hot spots) and low values (cold spots). It goes beyond simple density mapping by determining where features with high or low values cluster spatially in a way that is unlikely to result from random chance.

supporting_papers.paper_file: Song J, Zhu X and Yu X (2023) Monitoring and mapping vegetation greenery in resi
supporting_papers.citation: Song, J., Zhu, X., & Yu, X. (2023). Monitoring and mapping vegetation greenery in residential communities using street view images and a Normalized Vegetation Greenery Index: a case study in Beijing, China. Frontiers in Forests and Global Change, 6, 1071569.

MTH_HOT.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_CLA.name: Classification Analysis
MTH_CLA.definition: A supervised machine learning or statistical procedure used to categorize data into distinct, predefined classes. In urban research, this is often applied to land-use data, satellite imagery, or survey results to predict categorical outcomes (e.g., residential vs. commercial, high-risk vs. low-risk) based on input feature vectors.

supporting_papers.citation: Liu, W., Hu, X., Song, Z., & Yuan, X. (2023). Identifying the integrated visual characteristics of greenway landscape: A focus on human perception. Sustainable Cities and Society, 99, 104937.

MTH_CLA.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_KMN.name: K-Means Clustering
MTH_KMN.definition: A popular vector quantization and unsupervised learning algorithm that partitions a dataset into 'K' distinct, non-overlapping subgroups (clusters). It operates by iteratively assigning data points to the nearest cluster centroid and minimizing the within-cluster sum of squares. It is widely used for market segmentation and identifying urban functional zones.

supporting_papers.citation: Wang, M., Haworth, J., Chen, H., Liu, Y., & Shi, Z. (2024). Investigating the potential of crowdsourced street-level imagery in understanding the spatiotemporal dynamics of cities: A case study of walkability in Inner London. Cities, 153, 105243.

MTH_KMN.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_PSP.name: PSPNet (Pyramid Scene Parsing Network)
MTH_PSP.definition: PSPNet is a deep-learning semantic segmentation architecture using pyramid pooling to capture multi-scale context. Commonly used in street view image analysis to segment vegetation, buildings, sky, etc.

supporting_papers.citation: Li, B., Xing, H., Cao, D., Yang, G., & Zhang, H. (2022). Exploring the Effects of Roadside Vegetation on the Urban Thermal Environment Using Street View Images. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 19(3), 1272.

MTH_PSP.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_PDL.name: Panoptic-DeepLab Segmentation
MTH_PDL.definition: Panoptic-DeepLab unifies semantic and instance segmentation in a bottom-up panoptic framework, often used for urban scene parsing.

supporting_papers.citation: Qin, X., Yang, D., & Wangari, V. W. (2024). Quantitative characterization and evaluation of highway greening landscape spatial quality based on deep learning. Environmental Impact Assessment Review, 107, 107559.

MTH_PDL.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_IMG_HST.name: Image Histogram
MTH_IMG_HST.definition: A graphical representation of the tonal distribution in a digital image. It plots the number of pixels for each tonal value (intensity). In image processing and remote sensing, histogram analysis is a fundamental method used for thresholding, contrast adjustment, and analyzing the overall brightness and contrast of the captured scene to identify features.

supporting_papers.citation: Zang, P., Liu, X., Zhao, Y., Guo, H., Lu, Y., & Xue, C. Q. L. (2020). Eye-Level Street Greenery and Walking Behaviors of Older Adults. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 17(17), 6130.

MTH_IMG_HST.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_SLM.name: Spatial Lag Model
MTH_SLM.definition: A statistical method used in spatial econometrics and remote sensing analysis. The Spatial Lag Model accounts for spatial dependence by including a spatially lagged dependent variable, acknowledging that the value of a variable at one location is influenced by the values of the same variable at neighboring locations. This is crucial for analyzing geospatial data where observations are not independent.

supporting_papers.citation: Wang, R., Feng, Z., Pearce, J., Yao, Y., Li, X., & Liu, Y. (2021). The distribution of greenspace quantity and quality and their association with neighbourhood socioeconomic conditions in Guangzhou, China: A new approach using deep learning method and street view images. Sustainable Cities and Society, 66, 102664.

MTH_SLM.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_GLC.name: Global Land Cover
MTH_GLC.definition: Refers to classification methodologies used to map the biophysical cover over the Earth's surface. In the context of algorithms, it often refers to standardized classification schemes or specific datasets (like GLC2000) used as a method to categorize image pixels into classes such as forest, agriculture, water, and urban areas.
MTH_GLC.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_MRC.name: Mask R-CNN Instance Segmentation
MTH_MRC.definition: Mask R-CNN is a deep-learning instance segmentation framework extending Faster R-CNN with a per-region segmentation mask branch. Used for object-level urban analysis.

supporting_papers.citation: Qiu, W., Li, W., Liu, X., & Huang, X. (2022). Subjectively Measured Streetscape Qualities for Shanghai with Large-Scale Application of Computer Vision and Machine Learning. Proceedings of the 2021 DigitalFUTURES, 242–251.

MTH_MRC.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_SEG_NET.name: SegNet (Semantic Segmentation Network)
MTH_SEG_NET.definition: A deep convolutional encoder-decoder architecture specifically designed for semantic pixel-wise image segmentation. The core method involves an encoder network to generate feature maps and a corresponding decoder network that upsamples these maps to produce a dense classification output, widely used for understanding scene geometry and content in autonomous systems.

Zhou, H., Tao, G., Yan, X., & Sun, J. (2021). Influences of greening and structures on urban thermal environments: A case study in Xuzhou City, China. Urban Forestry & Urban Greening, 66, 127386.

MTH_SEG_NET.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_VDVI.name: Visible Difference Vegetation Index
MTH_VDVI.definition: A vegetation index designed for processing imagery containing only the visible spectrum (Red, Green, Blue), useful for low-cost UAV sensors lacking a Near-Infrared band. It is calculated as (2*Green - Red - Blue) / (2*Green + Red + Blue) to distinguish green vegetation from soil and other non-plant features.

supporting_papers.citation: Xue, C., Jin, C., Zhou, L., & Li, G. (2022). Exploring the distribution of city street greenery from eye-level: An application of Baidu Map panoramic images data. Geografisk Tidsskrift-Danish Journal of Geography, 122(1), 73–86.

MTH_VDVI.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_GSV.name: Google Street View
MTH_GSV.definition: Google Street View is identified as an online street view service that provides an enormous number of images at the street level, functioning as an electronic map based on actual scenery. The text highlights that the emergence of such services has shattered previous restrictions on data availability for evaluating streetscapes. Along with similar services like Tencent Street View, it offers images rich in detail with wide coverage, serving as a vital data source for evaluating the visual perception of urban streets.

supporting_papers.citation: Li, X., Ratti, C., & Seiferling, I. (2017). Mapping Urban Landscapes Along Streets Using Google Street View. Lecture Notes in Geoinformation and Cartography, 341–356.

O'Regan, A. C., Byrne, R., Hellebust, S., & Nyhan, M. M. (2022). Associations between Google Street View-derived urban greenspace metrics and air pollution measured using a distributed sensor network. Sustainable Cities and Society, 87, 104221.
Larkin, A., & Hystad, P. (2019). Evaluating street view exposure measures of visible green space for health research. Journal of Exposure Science & Environmental Epidemiology, 29(4), 447–456.

MTH_GSV.source_paper: Liang Cheng et al. - 2017 - Use of Tencent Street View Imagery for Visual Percep

MTH_JPG.name: Joint Photographic Experts Group (Compression)
MTH_JPG.definition: While commonly known as a file format, in a methodological context, this refers to the lossy compression algorithm defined by the committee. It utilizes the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) to reduce image file size by discarding high-frequency information that is less perceptible to the human eye, which impacts the spectral fidelity of images used for analysis.
MTH_JPG.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_SEG_COL.name: Segmentation by Color
MTH_SEG_COL.definition: While not explicitly named 'Segmentation by Color,' the text describes this method in the context of calculating the green view index. It involves analyzing street view images by counting the proportion of specific colored pixels—specifically green-colored pixels—to determine the value of a visual index. This approach allows for the quantitative assessment of landscape elements (such as vegetation) by isolating and measuring image data based on chromatic properties within the photographic samples.

supporting_papers.citation: Cheng, L., Chu, S., Zong, W., Li, S., Wu, J., & Li, M. (2017). Use of Tencent Street View Imagery for Visual Perception of Streets. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 6(9), 265.

MTH_SEG_COL.source_paper: Liang Cheng et al. - 2017 - Use of Tencent Street View Imagery for Visual Percep

MTH_OCV.name: OpenCV (Open Source Computer Vision Library)
MTH_OCV.definition: An open-source computer vision and machine learning software library (cv2/OpenCV). Provides functions for image processing, feature extraction, geometric transformation, color space conversion, and other visual analysis tasks commonly used in street-view image analysis workflows.

supporting_papers.citation: Nishio, S., & Ito, F. (2019). Application of Method for Calculating Sky View Factor Using Google Street View: Relation Between Sky View Factor and Physical Elements in Urban Space. Proceedings of the ICA, 2, 1–8.

MTH_OCV.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_SEG.name: Image Segmentation
MTH_SEG.definition: In the context of this study, image segmentation is described as a specific image processing method within a technical procedure designed to extract characteristics from large quantities of street view images. It is applied to panoramic images that have been processed to be compatible with human visual perspectives. This technique is utilized in conjunction with the detection of salient regions to automate the information acquisition required for determining the visual perception of streets.

supporting_papers.citation: Kang, Y., Zhang, F., Gao, S., Lin, H., & Liu, Y. (2020). A review of urban physical environment sensing using street view imagery in public health studies. Annals of GIS, 26(3), 261–275.

Kawshalya, L. W. G., Weerasinghe, U. G. D., & Chandrasekara, D. P. (2022). The impact of visual complexity on perceived safety and comfort of the users: A study on urban streetscape of Sri Lanka. PLOS ONE, 17(8), e0272074.

MTH_SEG.source_paper: Liang Cheng et al. - 2017 - Use of Tencent Street View Imagery for Visual Percep

MTH_M2F.name: Mask2Former
MTH_M2F.definition: A universal image segmentation architecture capable of addressing semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation tasks simultaneously. It utilizes a Masked-attention Mask Transformer to extract localized features by constraining cross-attention within predicted mask regions, significantly improving performance on difficult segmentation tasks compared to specialized models.

supporting_papers.citation: Sánchez, I. A. V., & Labib, S. M. (2024). Accessing eye-level greenness visibility from open-source street view images: A methodological development and implementation in multi-city and multi-country contexts. Sustainable Cities and Society, 103, 105262.

MTH_M2F.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_MSK.name: Mask R-CNN (Mask Region-based Convolutional Neural Network)
MTH_MSK.definition: A deep neural network architecture used for instance segmentation. It extends the Faster R-CNN object detection model by adding a parallel branch for predicting segmentation masks on each Region of Interest (RoI), allowing the model to detect objects and simultaneously generate a high-quality pixel-level mask for each instance.
MTH_MSK.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_BOX.name: Boxplot / Distribution Visualisation
MTH_BOX.definition: Five-number-summary visualisation showing median, quartiles, and outliers; used descriptively for indicator distributions.

supporting_papers.citation: Liu, Z., Ma, X., Hu, L., Liu, Y., Lu, S., Chen, H., & Tan, Z. (2022). Nonlinear Cooling Effect of Street Green Space Morphology: Evidence from a Gradient Boosting Decision Tree and Explainable Machine Learning Approach. Land, 11(12), 2220.

MTH_BOX.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_GIN.name: Gini Inequality Coefficient
MTH_GIN.definition: Statistical inequality measure between 0 (perfect equality) and 1 (perfect inequality), used to quantify spatial inequity of greenery, services, or visual quality.

supporting_papers.citation: Guo, X., Liu, C., Bi, S., & Tang, Y. (2025). Identification of Inequities in Green Visibility and Ways to Increase Greenery in Neighborhoods: A Case Study of Wuhan, China. Applied Sciences, 15(2), 742.

MTH_GIN.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_FCN.name: Fully Convolutional Network
MTH_FCN.definition: A deep learning architecture commonly used for semantic segmentation. Unlike traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) that end with fully connected layers for classification, FCNs consist entirely of convolutional layers. This allows the network to accept input images of arbitrary sizes and produce output maps of corresponding dimensions.

supporting_papers.citation: Rui, Q., & Cheng, H. (2023). Quantifying the spatial quality of urban streets with open street view images: A case study of the main urban area of Fuzhou. Ecological Indicators, 156, 111204.

Wang, K., Fang, X., Ma, Y., Xue, S., & Yin, S. (2022). Assessing Effects of Urban Greenery on the Regulation Mechanism of Microclimate and Outdoor Thermal Comfort during Winter in China's Cold Region. Land, 11(9), 1442.

MTH_FCN.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_PAN_FPN.name: Panoptic Feature Pyramid Network
MTH_PAN_FPN.definition: A computer vision architecture designed for panoptic segmentation, which unifies semantic segmentation (identifying background 'stuff') and instance segmentation (identifying distinct 'things'). It employs a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) backbone to extract multi-scale features, enabling robust detection and segmentation of objects across varying sizes and resolutions.

supporting_papers.citation: Zhang, Y., Li, S., Dong, R., Deng, H., Fu, X., Wang, C., Yu, T., Jia, T., & Zhao, J. (2021). Quantifying physical and psychological perceptions of urban scenes using deep learning. Land Use Policy, 111, 105762.

MTH_PAN_FPN.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_SGF.name: Savitzky-Golay Filter
MTH_SGF.definition: Smoothing filter that fits a low-order polynomial in a moving window; used to reduce noise in time-series or angular profiles.

supporting_papers.citation: Ogawa, Y., Oki, T., Zhao, C., Sekimoto, Y., & Shimizu, C. (2024). Evaluating the subjective perceptions of streetscapes using street-view images. Landscape and Urban Planning, 247, 105073.

MTH_SGF.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_IDW.name: Inverse Distance Weighting
MTH_IDW.definition: A deterministic spatial interpolation method used to estimate values at unknown locations based on known sample points. It operates on the assumption that things that are closer to one another are more alike than those that are farther apart; therefore, the influence of a known point on an interpolated value diminishes as the distance between them increases.

supporting_papers.citation: Meng, Y., Xing, H., Yuan, Y., Wong, M. S., & Fan, K. (2020). Sensing urban poverty: From the perspective of human perception-based greenery and open-space landscapes. Computers, Environment and Urban Systems, 84, 101544.

MTH_IDW.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_HSI.name: HSI Colour Space Thresholding
MTH_HSI.definition: Image segmentation method that converts RGB to HSI (Hue-Saturation-Intensity) colour space and applies hue/saturation thresholds to identify green pixels.

supporting_papers.citation: Dong, R., Zhang, Y., & Zhao, J. (2018). How Green Are the Streets Within the Sixth Ring Road of Beijing? An Analysis Based on Tencent Street View Pictures and the Green View Index. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 15(7), 1367.

MTH_HSI.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_EWM.name: Entropy Weight Method
MTH_EWM.definition: An objective weighting method used in multi-criteria decision analysis and statistics. It determines the weight of various indicators by calculating their information entropy, which measures the amount of useful information provided by the data. A lower entropy value implies greater variation and thus a higher weight in the evaluation model.

supporting_papers.citation: Tao, Y., Wang, Y., Wang, X., Tian, G., & Zhang, S. (2022). Measuring the Correlation between Human Activity Density and Streetscape Perceptions: An Analysis Based on Baidu Street View Images in Zhengzhou, China. Land, 11(3), 400.

MTH_EWM.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_BSV.name: Baidu Street View Imagery Analysis
MTH_BSV.definition: A geospatial research method utilizing panoramic 360-degree street-level imagery provided by Baidu Maps. In urban analytics and environmental studies, this method is employed to extract visual features of the built environment—such as green view indices, sky openness, and building facades—often using computer vision techniques to assess urban quality, walkability, and spatial characteristics in regions where Google Street View is unavailable.

MTH_BSV.review_note: Audited after provisional review; definition accepted as valid.

MTH_HSV.name: Hue, Saturation, Value Color Model
MTH_HSV.definition: A color representation method used in image processing and computer vision that defines colors based on three components: Hue (color type), Saturation (intensity/purity), and Value (brightness). Unlike the RGB model, HSV separates chromatic information from luma (intensity), making it a preferred method for color-based segmentation and feature extraction in scene analysis, as it is more robust to changes in external lighting conditions.

MTH_MED_ANL.name: Mediation Analysis
MTH_MED_ANL.definition: A statistical method used to elucidate the mechanism underlying an observed relationship between an independent variable and a dependent variable via the inclusion of a third explanatory variable, known as a mediator. This analysis seeks to identify the extent to which the effect of the independent variable is direct versus how much is transmitted indirectly through the mediator, offering deeper insights into causal pathways.

SRC_FLD.name: Field Survey
SRC_FLD.definition: SRC_FLD involves the direct observation and collection of data within the actual physical environment rather than through remote sensing or simulations. This method encompasses on-site measurements, photography, observation of user behavior, and physical audits of site conditions. Field surveys provide ground-truth data that captures the nuances of the genius loci, sensory inputs (sound, smell), and ephemeral conditions that laboratory or digital analyses might miss. It ensures the validity of the dataset by verifying real-world conditions against theoretical models.
